primary_diagnosis.name: Carcinoid syndrome (metastatic neuroendocrine tumour) with carcinoid heart disease — acute alcohol-triggered exacerbation with bronchospasm

comorbidities.name: Carcinoid heart disease (tricuspid regurgitation, right-heart plaque)

pathophysiology_summary: A neuroendocrine (carcinoid) tumour — classically midgut, and symptomatic once hepatic metastases allow vasoactive mediators to bypass first-pass hepatic clearance — secretes serotonin, histamine, tachykinins, kallikrein and prostaglandins into the systemic circulation. These mediators produce the syndrome's features: episodic cutaneous flushing (vasodilation), secretory watery diarrhoea (increased gut motility and secretion), and bronchospasm. Chronic serotonin exposure drives fibrous plaque deposition on the right-sided (tricuspid and pulmonary) valves, producing carcinoid heart disease (tricuspid regurgitation, raised JVP, peripheral oedema) and eventual right heart failure. Mediator release is provoked by triggers including alcohol, tyramine-rich foods, physical stress and — critically — catecholamines. A carcinoid crisis is a life-threatening surge of mediator release causing profound flushing, bronchospasm and haemodynamic instability; it can be precipitated by exogenous catecholamines such as adrenaline, which is why treating this presentation as anaphylaxis with adrenaline is dangerous. Definitive management is supportive stabilisation plus a somatostatin analogue (e.g. octreotide) in hospital, with biochemical confirmation by elevated 24-hour urinary 5-HIAA and chromogranin A, and tumour localisation by imaging.

differential.evidence_basis: Acute flushing, bronchospasm and GI symptoms after alcohol superficially fit anaphylaxis and MUST be excluded first as the immediate life threat.
New wheeze and breathlessness — but does not explain the intense flushing or profuse secretory diarrhoea.
Watery diarrhoea — but does not explain the vasomotor flushing or the respiratory wheeze.
The recurrent, episodic, multi-system vasomotor pattern (flushing + secretory diarrhoea + bronchospasm) is the diagnostic tetrad, but is easily missed at first pass.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early on, excluding anaphylaxis is the correct safety priority; the model should not be penalised for considering it, but should keep a broad differential given the recurrent history.

differential.evidence_basis: Recurrent episodic flushing + secretory diarrhoea + new bronchospasm, alcohol-triggered, with chronic right-heart features and weight loss — the carcinoid pattern.
No urticaria/angioedema, no allergen or new-food exposure, recurrent episodic history — makes true anaphylaxis unlikely, though it remains the must-exclude emergency.
Episodic flushing, diarrhoea and bronchospasm with alcohol as a trigger can also occur in mast cell disease — a genuine biochemical differential.
Isolated lower-airway asthma cannot explain flushing, secretory diarrhoea or the right-heart findings.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The pivot from the anaphylaxis mimic to a recurrent systemic vasomotor syndrome is the core diagnostic move being tested.

differential.diagnosis: Carcinoid syndrome with carcinoid heart disease
differential.evidence_basis: Full tetrad plus tricuspid regurgitation/raised JVP and weight loss; requires urinary 5-HIAA, chromogranin A and imaging for confirmation — cannot be confirmed via telehealth.
Excluded on history: recurrent episodic pattern, no allergen, no urticaria/angioedema — but adrenaline must still be avoided because it can precipitate a carcinoid crisis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor cannot make the diagnosis remotely; the correct terminal position is a strong provisional recognition plus urgent escalation and specialist workup.

red_flags.description: Acute bronchospasm with respiratory distress and hypoxia (fast, laboured, wheezy breathing)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Airway/breathing compromise is the immediate threat and drives emergency-department escalation; hypoxia on room air indicates significant gas-exchange impairment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive respiratory failure if the bronchospasm and mediator surge are not treated in hospital.

red_flags.description: High-volume ongoing secretory diarrhoea with dehydration and electrolyte-loss risk
red_flags.clinical_significance: Continuous secretory losses can cause hypovolaemia and electrolyte derangement, compounding haemodynamic instability — needs IV fluids and bloods in hospital.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Hypovolaemic collapse and dangerous electrolyte disturbance.

red_flags.description: Signs of right-heart involvement (chronic ankle oedema, raised JVP, tricuspid regurgitation murmur) — carcinoid heart disease
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates established carcinoid heart disease and the risk of impending right-sided heart failure; the murmur and JVP are clinician findings the patient cannot report directly.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-appreciation of cardiac risk and delayed cardiology/specialist involvement.

red_flags.description: Haemodynamic instability / carcinoid-crisis potential (fluctuating BP, tachycardia, vasomotor surge)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A vasomotor surge can progress to carcinoid crisis with cardiovascular collapse; exogenous catecholamines (adrenaline) can precipitate it — a key management pitfall.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Cardiovascular collapse, worsened if adrenaline is administered as for anaphylaxis.

red_flags.description: Unintentional weight loss on a background of recurrent episodic symptoms
red_flags.clinical_significance: Constitutional red flag pointing to an underlying neoplastic process requiring urgent specialist workup.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed diagnosis of an underlying neuroendocrine tumour.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Locking onto anaphylaxis because of flushing + bronchospasm + alcohol trigger, and missing the recurrent episodic multi-system pattern that points to a neuroendocrine cause.
Treating the picture as anaphylaxis and advising/giving adrenaline — a catecholamine that can precipitate a life-threatening carcinoid crisis in this patient.
Attributing the wheeze to asthma alone and ignoring the flushing, secretory diarrhoea and right-heart signs that make it systemic.
Labelling the diarrhoea as a self-limiting gut infection and under-triaging, missing the vasomotor and respiratory components.

Attempting to 'confirm' carcinoid syndrome or manage it remotely — it needs urinary 5-HIAA, chromogranin A, echocardiography and imaging that telehealth cannot provide; the correct move is recognition plus escalation.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Auscultated expiratory wheeze and true air entry
Holosystolic tricuspid regurgitation murmur (carcinoid heart disease)
Exact blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate and SpO2 (no home device offered)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise an acute systemic vasomotor exacerbation with respiratory distress and ongoing fluid loss; arrange urgent same-hour emergency-department assessment for respiratory support, IV fluids, electrolyte correction and specialist (endocrine/oncology) workup. Instruct the partner to call 000 immediately if breathing becomes unmanageable, the lips turn blue, or the patient becomes confused or drowsy. Explicitly AVOID adrenaline.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The main danger is not under-diagnosis of the zebra but MIS-treatment of the mimic — anchoring on anaphylaxis and giving adrenaline (which can precipitate carcinoid crisis), or on asthma/gastroenteritis and under-escalating.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Carcinoid syndrome is confirmed biochemically (24-hour urinary 5-HIAA, chromogranin A) and by imaging; somatostatin analogues (octreotide) control mediator-driven symptoms and are used to prevent/treat carcinoid crisis. Avoid catecholamines, which can precipitate crisis.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines 2023
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Used here to EXCLUDE anaphylaxis: absence of urticaria/angioedema, no allergen exposure, and a recurrent episodic pattern argue against true anaphylaxis, though it remains the must-exclude emergency.
